explanation: CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer, the design style with many multi-step instructions and variable-length encodings (x86). Its counterpart is RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer), with few fixed-length, single-cycle instructions.

NAND is functionally complete (a universal gate): NOT a = NAND(a,a), AND = NAND followed by that inverter, and OR = NAND of the two inverted inputs by De Morgan. AND or OR alone cannot produce inversion, so no set of only-AND or only-OR gates can implement every combinational function. NOR is also universal but is not offered here.

A multiplexer has 2^n data inputs plus n select lines and routes exactly one of them to a single output. The trap is the inverse circuits: a demultiplexer and a decoder take one input to 2^n outputs, and an encoder takes 2^n inputs to n outputs, not to one line.

The physical address is the real location in main memory that the memory unit actually sees on the address bus. A logical (virtual) address is what the CPU generates; the MMU translates it into a physical address at run time.

A flip-flop is the basic bistable element, holding exactly one bit. Register is the classic trap: a register is a group of n flip-flops and stores n bits, so it is not the one-bit storage circuit. Decoders and encoders are combinational and store nothing.
A DBMS is the software layer that stores, modifies and retrieves data on behalf of users and applications. It is not a language: SQL is the language, and the DBMS is the system that executes it.

Hierarchical, network and distributed are all standard database classifications; "decentralized" is not part of the usual taxonomy, so it is the odd one out. Weak item: the distinction between decentralized and distributed is terminology, not reasoning, so learn the keyed list rather than trying to derive it.

The standard component list for a DBMS covers data, the data languages (DDL, DML, DCL), the data manager (database engine), plus hardware, users and procedures. All three listed items are components, so "All of the above" is correct.

Querying rows plus INSERT, DELETE and UPDATE are all DML operations, because they act on the tuples inside a table. The distractors act elsewhere: DDL defines the schema (CREATE, ALTER, DROP), DCL handles privileges (GRANT, REVOKE), and TCL handles transactions (COMMIT, ROLLBACK).
DML = Data Manipulation Language (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).

Answer unverified: this item is defective as printed and the source supplies no key. Minimum duplication/redundancy (A), high security (B) and ACID support (D) are all genuine DBMS features, so three options are true at once, while only C ("Single-user access only") is false. That 3-true/1-false pattern means the stem lost a negation: the original almost certainly read "Which of the following is NOT a feature of DBMS?", keyed C. If a question like this appears, read it as the NOT version and pick single-user access.

Keyed B: providing a user/query interface is a general feature of a database system. A and C are anti-features (no backup, lack of authentication), and D describes distributed databases specifically rather than databases in general, which is the trap here.
RDBMS is the relational model, which organizes all data as a collection of tables (relations). Records, fields and keys are the parts inside one table, not what the system as a whole is composed of.
FAT = File Allocation Table, the on-disk structure that records which clusters belong to which file and chains them together.

All three are per-process resources: the process owns its address space and global variables, its own open-file table, and its own pending alarms, signals and signal handlers, so "all of the mentioned" is correct. The contrast to remember is threads, which share all of these within a single process and keep only their own stack, registers and program counter.
